meta.title: Greenways sustainability glossary
meta.description: Curated hospitality sustainability terms for agent explanations. Source: Sustainability tool kit.md (cleaned).

terms.short: Meeting today's needs without compromising future generations — balancing environmental protection, social fairness, and economic viability.
A framework measuring success across social impact, environmental impact, and financial performance — not profit alone.
Designing out waste by keeping materials and food in use longer (reuse, repair, repurpose, recycle) and regenerating natural systems.
terms.long: Highly relevant for packaging, food scraps, and kitchen fit-out — less landfill, more value from each input.
howToReduce: Separate organics for compost or AD

terms.short: Going beyond doing less harm to actively improving ecosystems — e.g. sourcing from farms that rebuild soil health and biodiversity.
Total greenhouse gas emissions caused directly and indirectly by your operation — cooking, energy, deliveries, waste, and purchased ingredients.
A unit that expresses all greenhouse gases (methane, nitrous oxide, etc.) as the equivalent amount of CO₂ so emissions can be added and compared.

Scope 1: direct emissions you control (gas boilers, owned vans). Scope 2: purchased electricity/heat. Scope 3: everything else in your value chain. For restaurants, Scope 3 is usually the largest share.
Indirect emissions linked to food, packaging, transport, waste, staff travel, and supplier activity — usually 90–99% of total emissions for restaurants.
terms.long: Scope 3 is the emissions that happen outside your kitchen: growing ingredients, making packaging, deliveries to you, takeaway logistics, and waste treatment. Credible plans tackle menu design, suppliers, and waste prevention first — not only gas and electricity in the building.
howToReduce: Shift menu toward lower-impact ingredients and portions
Cut food waste and improve forecasting
Engage suppliers on data and lower-impact options
Reduce packaging and delivery distance where practical

terms.short: Emission-reduction goals aligned with climate science (e.g. meaningful cuts by 2030) to stay within safe global warming limits.
Reducing emissions as much as possible first, then balancing only unavoidable remainder with high-quality removals — offsets are a last resort, not the plan.
Emissions linked to each dish based on ingredients, portions, and preparation — used to compare items and highlight lower-carbon choices.
howToReduce: Swap high-impact ingredients where quality allows

terms.short: Emissions from food, drink, cleaning products, and other supplies you buy — often the biggest Scope 3 category for restaurants.
howToReduce: Choose suppliers who share carbon data

terms.short: Emissions created before ingredients reach you — farming, processing, and manufacturing upstream of your kitchen.
howToReduce: Prefer renewable energy or regenerative practices

terms.short: Emissions from making bottles, cartons, boxes, trays, cups, and takeaway containers.
Emissions moving goods from farms and factories to your restaurant.
howToReduce: Avoid rush shipments with better ordering

terms.short: Food bought or prepared that is not eaten — prep waste, spoilage, and plate waste. Major cost and emissions issue (production emissions plus disposal).
howToReduce: Donate safe surplus where rules allow

terms.short: What happens after disposal — composting, anaerobic digestion, recycling, or landfill — each with different emissions.
howToReduce: Reduce waste created in the first place

terms.short: Emissions from third-party delivery, takeaway logistics, and catering transport.
Emissions from producing ovens, fridges, dishwashers, furniture, and fit-out — upfront but significant over asset life.
The part of your value chain with the biggest emissions impact — often beef, dairy, food waste, packaging, or delivery.
howToReduce: Focus on the largest hotspot first for fastest meaningful cuts

terms.short: Working with suppliers to improve data, reduce emissions, and improve sourcing — essential for credible Scope 3 cuts.
Missing or incomplete emissions information from suppliers or products — common early in the journey.
Buying ingredients close to where you operate and in season to cut transport emissions, support local producers, and improve freshness.
Menus designed to increase plants (vegetables, legumes, grains) and reduce reliance on animal products, especially high-impact meats.
A target to serve more plant protein than animal protein across the menu (e.g. 60% plant / 40% animal), aligned with planetary health guidance.
Menu items designed to stay under a set emissions limit and labelled to guide guest choices.
Using the whole animal or plant (offcuts, stems, peels) to minimise waste and get more value from each ingredient.
Menus that change frequently (weekly or daily) based on what is in season locally — less storage, transport, and waste.
Safe edible food that would otherwise be wasted but can be donated to charities or staff, following local rules.
Turning organic waste (peelings, coffee grounds, allowed food scraps) into soil amendment on-site or via collection.
Industrial process breaking down organic waste to produce biogas (energy) and digestate (fertiliser) — common in UK/EU waste systems.
An operation designed to send as little as possible to landfill/incineration through prevention, reuse, composting, and recycling.
Items like plastic cutlery, plates, straws, and some takeaway containers — many restricted in UK/EU, pushing reusables or alternatives.
Systems where customers or delivery partners use returnable containers cleaned and reused — cuts single-use waste.
Certification showing cleaning products, paper goods, or supplies meet strict environmental and health criteria.
Electricity or heat from wind, solar, hydro, etc. — switching supplier or adding on-site solar is a common quick win.
Using less energy for the same output — LED lighting, efficient fridges/ovens, insulation, smart controls. Cuts bills and emissions.
Reducing water use with low-flow fixtures, leak repair, full dishwasher loads, and staff practices.
Designing or refurbishing spaces with sustainable materials, efficient systems, and layouts that reduce waste and enable reuse.
Buying from suppliers with fair wages, animal welfare standards, and environmental protection.
Buying directly from local producers or via few intermediaries — more transparency, less transport, stronger local economies.
EU rule requiring certain companies (and parts of value chains) to report environmental, social, and governance impacts — affects larger groups and some suppliers to big brands.
Three pillars investors and regulators use to assess sustainability — emissions, labour, diversity, food safety, and governance for hospitality.
Sustainable Restaurant Association framework rating hospitality on sourcing, environment, society, and governance.
Michelin recognition for outstanding sustainability — local sourcing, waste reduction, community engagement.
Food produced without synthetic pesticides or fertilisers, with strict animal welfare and processing rules under national/EU bodies.
Label ensuring producers (often in the Global South) receive fair prices and better social/environmental conditions — common on coffee, tea, chocolate.
Marine Stewardship Council (wild-caught) and Aquaculture Stewardship Council (farmed) — seafood meeting sustainability standards.
Certification for crops like coffee and cocoa addressing biodiversity, climate, and farmer livelihoods.
Signs or icons showing low-carbon, plant-based, local/seasonal, or certified items to help guests choose.
Campaigns highlighting meat-free dishes on specific days to normalise lower-impact eating without removing choice.
Flexible or smaller portions and take-home options to reduce plate waste while maintaining satisfaction.
E-bikes, EVs, route optimisation, and incentives for nearby or off-peak orders to cut delivery emissions.
Simple measures such as kg food waste per 100 covers, % plant-based dishes sold, kWh per cover, litres water per cover, or % certified suppliers.
Estimating emissions per dish or menu using recognised methods, then setting targets and redesigning recipes.
Sharing progress and challenges with staff and guests through dashboards, menu notes, or short updates — builds trust.
Staff travel to work, supplier visits, and events — often smaller than food emissions but visible in reporting.
Emissions from delivering food to customers — takeaway, meal kits, catering — after it leaves your kitchen.
Emissions from landfill, incineration, recycling, composting, or AD after customers finish — including packaging.
